notes.anchor: From each horn of the harbour we are throwing out moles
notes.note: Caesar quotes his own engineering: to bottle Pompey up in Brundisium he built moles out from both arms ("horns") of the harbour mouth, joining them by rafts; the deep water defeated the work and Pompey escaped on the Ides of March.

notes.anchor: exacting vengeance for Cnaeus Carbo, for Marcus Brutus
notes.note: Cn. Papirius Carbo (consul 82 BC) and the elder M. Iunius Brutus were Marian leaders whom the young Pompey had hunted down and executed during Sulla's victory; Caesar is reported to be casting himself as the avenger of Pompey's Sullan-era killings.

notes.anchor: with this Pompey as his partner
notes.note: Caesar's charge is that Pompey had been the dictator Sulla's willing instrument in the proscriptions of 82--81 BC, earning the youthful nickname "the teenage butcher"; Caesar thus claims the moral high ground as restorer of Sulla's victims.

notes.anchor: he complains of Milo, driven out by violence
notes.note: T. Annius Milo had been convicted and exiled for the murder of Clodius in 52 BC, after Pompey rammed the prosecution through; the partisan injustice of that trial is now cited against the Pompeian side.

notes.anchor: This is from a certain Baebius
notes.note: An otherwise unknown informant who carried this report from Curio's camp; Cicero, suspicious of the man's reliability, breaks off with a damaged remark and declines to vouch for the gossip.

notes.anchor: on the seventh day before the Kalends of April
notes.note: By inclusive Roman reckoning backward from the Kalends (1st) of April, this is 26 March 49 BC --- the date Lepta's express gives for Caesar's expected arrival at Capua.
